defs: A borough; a manor; as, the Bury of St. Edmond's
notes: used as a termination of names of places; as, Canterbury, Shrewsbury.

quotes: To this very day, the chief house of a manor, or the lord's seat, is called bury, in some parts of England.

records.ety: [OE. burien, birien, berien, AS. byrgan; akin to beorgan to protect, OHG. bergan, G. bergen, Icel. bjarga, Sw. berga, Dan. bierge, Goth. bargan. √95. Cf. Burrow.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Buried (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Burying (<?/).]

defs: To cover out of sight, either by heaping something over, or by placing within something, as earth, etc.; to conceal by covering; to hide; as, to bury coals in ashes; to bury the face in the hands.
quotes: And all their confidence Under the weight of mountains buried deep.

defs: Specifically: To cover out of sight, as the body of a deceased person, in a grave, a tomb, or the ocean; to deposit (a corpse) in its resting place, with funeral ceremonies; to inter; to inhume.
quotes: Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father.
I'll bury thee in a triumphant grave.

defs: To hide in oblivion; to put away finally; to abandon; as, to bury strife.
quotes: Give me a bowl of wine In this I bury all unkindness, Cassius.
